text: Minority populations remain vastly underrepresented in clinical research, a disparity that has become increasingly critical as cancer burdens and chronic disease rates continue to climb. In the United States, recruitment efforts are frequently stymied by complex barriers, most notably a pervasive lack of trust in institutional medicine. Successful engagement requires a nuanced strategy that respects community history, cultural beliefs, and socioeconomic pressures. Over several decades of global research, we developed a methodology centered on recruiting and training local residents to serve as primary research staff and frontline communicators. Upon establishing our program in the Texas Rio Grande Valley in 2001, we applied this community-based model to the study of chronic disease. In 2004, we trained a dedicated team of local women who successfully enrolled a longitudinal cohort of over 5,000 Mexican Americans from randomly selected households. This team has since evolved into a highly skilled workforce capable of performing advanced diagnostics, including ultrasonography, FibroScan, retinal imaging, and cognitive assessments. Today, this infrastructure supports several major initiatives, including NIH-funded clinical trials and multi-omic studies of liver disease, obesity, and diabetes. By leveraging local expertise, we are advancing precision medicine approaches to improve the prevention and treatment of chronic diseases in underserved populations.
